en.changeSummary: {ch1}The afternoon storm picture has intensified significantly — what was forecast as ordinary rain is now producing a severe thunderstorm with large hail, very heavy rain, and strong wind gusts to {q:gust:41}. {ch2}The severe hazard flag has been raised for the midday-to-evening block; exercise caution outdoors and stay aware of any official warnings.
en.closing: Conditions are expected to ease somewhat after this evening, with overcast skies and a return to lighter rain and winds overnight. Tomorrow brings a warming trend with notably higher storm energy, so keep an eye on further updates for Wednesday.

input.reasoning_trace:
Step 1 — TAF vs GFS: The TAF issuance (2026-04-21T11:38Z) is newer than the GFS run (2026-04-21T06:00Z), so the TAF prevails within its validity window. The TAF base period shows overcast/showers for 12–18Z, with a PROB30 of thunderstorms 19–23Z, broadly consistent with GFS rain. However, the current observation (Step 2) overrides this for the earliest block.

Step 2 — Observation override: The 13:53Z METAR (within the 12–18Z block) reports a severe thunderstorm in progress — heavy thunderstorm, rain, and hail, CB at 2,500 ft, winds 230° at 25 mph (~22 kt) gusting 47 mph (~41 kt), visibility sharply reduced. This directly overrides the 12–18Z block. The observation replaces the rain-only provisional with thunderstorm + severeFlag=true for that block. Winds updated to min 18 kt / max 26 kt (sustained observation), with gust noted in quantities. The 18–00Z block is left as likely/rain per TAF and GFS, no severe signal.

Step 3 — Diff vs prior_snapshot: Prior committed forecast showed precipPhenomenon=rain, severeFlag=false for the 12–18Z block. The reconciled block now shows precipPhenomenon=thunderstorm, severeFlag=true, with damaging gusts and hail actively occurring. This is a safety-critical upgrade (severe thunderstorm now in progress where plain rain was promised) and warrants an immediate unscheduled send. Although changed_since_last_sent_report is "observation only," the directional asymmetry rule applies — weather significantly worse than promised has arrived, making this genuinely newsworthy. The anti-reversal rule does not apply here because we are escalating (not reversing) a hazard level based on observed conditions already present.
